title: Gold Rush
description: California Gold Rush and related mining activity

title: Water History
description: Water infrastructure, aqueducts, dams, and water rights in SCV

title: Indigenous History
description: History of Native peoples of the Santa Clarita Valley and surrounding region, including Tataviam (primary SCV territory), Chumash (coastal/mission connections), Tongva/Gabrielino (LA Basin/mission connections), Serrano (northern/eastern border), Kitanemuk (Tehachapi border), and Vanyume (Mojave/Antelope Valley). CARE Principles and PNAAM apply for all Indigenous content.

title: Railroads
description: Railroad development and impact on SCV

title: Oil Industry
description: Oil discovery, drilling, and refining in SCV — first commercially productive oil well in California at Pico Canyon

title: Film Industry
description: Motion picture production and filming locations in SCV

title: Agriculture
description: Farming, ranching, and agriculture in SCV

title: Architecture
description: Historic buildings and architectural styles in SCV

title: Military History
description: Military events, bases, and service in SCV

title: Politics and Government
description: Local, state, and federal politics in SCV

title: Law Enforcement
description: Police, sheriffs, and law enforcement in SCV

title: Education
description: Schools, colleges, and educational institutions in SCV

title: Religion
description: Religious institutions and practices in SCV

title: Commerce and Trade
description: Business, retail, and commerce in SCV

title: Land Grants
description: Mexican and Spanish land grants and ranchos

title: Natural Disasters
description: Floods, fires, earthquakes, and other disasters in SCV

title: Conservation
description: Environmental and historic preservation in SCV

title: Arts and Culture
description: Visual arts, performing arts, and cultural heritage in SCV

title: Tataviam History
description: Specific history and culture of the Tataviam people (Fernandeño Tataviam Band of Mission Indians). No exact sacred-site coordinates published. CARE Principles apply.

title: Californio History
description: Californio community and culture in California and SCV

title: Chumash History
description: History and culture of the Chumash people (Santa Ynez Band of Chumash Indians and related bands) of the California coast, with regional connections to SCV via missions, trade networks, and the 1824 Chumash revolt. Scope: contextual/historical for SCV — primary Chumash territory is coastal California.

title: Tongva History
description: History and culture of the Tongva (Gabrielino/Gabrieleño/Kizh) people of the Los Angeles Basin and Southern Channel Islands (Tovaangar), with regional connections to SCV via missions and trade. No federal recognition as of 2026 — multiple groups petitioning. CARE Principles apply. NOTE: Wikidata QID requires verification — Q24251468 confirmed as Chumash, not Tongva.

title: Serrano History
description: History and culture of the Serrano (Maarrenga'yam / Vanyume) people of the San Bernardino Mountains, Antelope Valley, and Mojave Desert, with regional connections to SCV via trade, missions, and border territories. Primary authority: San Manuel Band of Mission Indians (federally recognized). CARE Principles apply.

title: Kitanemuk History
description: History and culture of the Kitanemuk people of the Tehachapi Mountains and Antelope Valley, with regional connections to SCV via trade and border territories. No standalone federally recognized tribe — descendants affiliated with San Manuel Band (Serrano) or Tataviam groups. CARE Principles apply.

title: Vanyume History
description: History and culture of the Vanyume, a Serrano subgroup of the Mojave Desert and Antelope Valley. Primary authority: San Manuel Band of Mission Indians. CARE Principles apply.

title: Spanish Colonial Expedition
description: Spanish exploratory and military expeditions in Alta California, including the Portolá Expedition of 1769. Content framed in context of Indigenous impact and colonization, respectful of Tataviam, Serrano, Tongva, and other Native perspectives.
